text: The patient is positioned on his side with the right side down, revealing the spleen pushed to the left side. A 12mm port is used for the camera, and an additional 12mm port along with two 5mm ports are inserted to facilitate tissue dissection.
The surgeon uses an angled scope to take down the tissues holding the splenic flexure of the colon against the spleen, mobilizing the colon away from the spleen.
A five millimeter port is inserted in the flank to aid in retracting the spleen and to facilitate the passage of instruments at various angles during the laparoscopic splenectomy.

The surgeon lifts the very large spleen from its inferior edge, revealing early attachments around the splenic hilum. The lino-renal ligament, which connects the spleen to the kidney, is identified and dissected using a harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon uses an additional port to insert an instrument that divides the fatty tissue running to the inferior pole of the large spleen, which is being lifted and stabilized for dissection.

The surgeon uses the N-seal bipolar instrument to divide and ligate small tributaries of the spleen, effectively sealing the vessels and mobilizing fat away from the inferior pole. The tissue between the spleen and the stomach is exposed, revealing the hilum of the spleen where the blood vessels enter.

The ENSEAL device is used to open the fat between the spleen and the stomach, exposing the lesser sac behind the stomach. The surgeon carefully divides the short gastric vessels running between the spleen and stomach, ensuring no damage to the stomach or bleeding. A posterior short gastric vessel remains visible, which is securely divided using the ENSEAL device.

The surgeon divides extra tissue near the upper pole to further separate the stomach from the spleen and expose the hilum of the spleen. The peritoneum over the hilum is dissected and divided, revealing the splenic artery. Dissection continues, opening a wide area and revealing the tail of the pancreas.

The surgeon uses an energy device to carefully divide fatty tissue away from the pancreas, avoiding proximity to the pancreas itself. The capsule of the hilum of the spleen and the peritoneum investing the surrounding fat are dissected to expose the large blood vessels entering the spleen. The splenic artery is controlled first to decompress the spleen and facilitate its removal.

The surgeon identifies the bifurcation of the splenic artery, carefully dissecting the vessel while preserving the pancreas. The laparoscopic view reveals the deep abdominal cavity with the spleen forming the upper boundary, highlighting the complexity of performing a splenectomy on a large spleen laparoscopically.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the inferior aspect of the splenic vein, ensuring not to damage any potential pancreatic tissue that may be wrapped around the vessel. Fatty tissue adjacent to the artery is cleaned, and the vessel running into the spleen is gradually mobilized using a stepwise approach to ensure safe dissection.

The surgeon carefully separates the splenic artery and vein, avoiding direct manipulation of the artery to prevent tearing. Nerves and lymphatics surrounding the artery are meticulously dissected away, exposing a sufficient length of the artery for ligation.

The surgeon uses metallic clips to ligate the splenic artery, placing an extra clip for added security to prevent rapid bleeding. The fat surrounding the splenic vein is further mobilized with caution to avoid damaging any pancreatic tissue. The spleen is gradually decompressing through the vein, while the posterior hiloperitoneum is further divided.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the splenic vein, ensuring no pancreatic tissue is damaged. The clips are removed from the spleen side of the artery using an end seal to seal the artery, preparing for the use of a stapler.

The surgeon introduces a vascular stapler to staple the splenic vein, ensuring the staple is securely across the vein. The camera view flickers due to instrument clashes in the confined space. With the hilum fully dissected, the surgeon focuses on the liana renal ligament, rolling the patient forward to facilitate the spleen's movement. The final attachments between the spleen and diaphragm are severed using a harmonic scalpel, freeing the spleen completely.
The surgeon ensures the spleen is completely free by manipulating it before introducing an Alexis extraction bag through an enlarged trocar site.

The surgeon enlarges a trocar site from twelve to fifteen millimeters to pass the Alexis extraction bag. The semi-stiff plastic rim of the bag is positioned flat with the posterior aspect tucked behind the inferior pole of the spleen. The anterior part of the hoop is lifted to gradually encase the spleen, moving from the inferior to the superior pole. The camera is occasionally brushed by the bag during this process. The superior pole is tucked into the bag, and the ring is delivered outside the incision. A small hole in the bag allows for instruments to morselate the spleen, which is eventually removed without perforating the bag.
